title: Describe Sides, Vertices, and Angles of Polygons
essential_question: How can you describe a polygon by its sides, vertices, and angles?

A.note: Circles are not polygons — they have no straight sides

learn.title: What Is a Polygon?
learn.content: A POLYGON is a closed figure made of straight sides. Two rules: (1) it must be CLOSED — all sides connect with no gaps. (2) every side must be STRAIGHT — no curves allowed. Examples of polygons: a triangle, a square, a stop sign. NOT polygons: a circle (curved), a half-open shape (not closed).

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.13 Lesson — U13_shapes/L1_describe_polygons
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Sides of a Polygon
learn.content: Each STRAIGHT line segment that forms a polygon is called a SIDE. Count the sides by tracing around the polygon and counting each straight segment. A triangle has 3 sides. A square has 4 sides. A stop sign has 8 sides.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.13 Lesson — U13_shapes/L1_describe_polygons
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Vertices — The Corners
learn.content: A VERTEX is the point where two sides meet — what you might call a CORNER. The plural is VERTICES. A triangle has 3 vertices (3 corners). A pentagon has 5 vertices. Important rule: the number of vertices ALWAYS equals the number of sides.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.13 Lesson — U13_shapes/L1_describe_polygons
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015
verification.original_ref: Go Math 12.1: vertex definition. Sides = vertices.

learn.title: Angles — Inside Each Corner
learn.content: An ANGLE is formed at each vertex by the two sides that meet there. Every polygon has the SAME number of angles as sides. A triangle has 3 angles. A hexagon has 6 angles. So for ANY polygon: number of sides = number of vertices = number of angles.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.13 Lesson — U13_shapes/L1_describe_polygons
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Right Angles — The Square Corners
learn.content: A RIGHT ANGLE looks like the corner of a book, a window, or a piece of paper. It is a perfect square corner. Squares and rectangles have 4 right angles each. Some polygons have NO right angles (like a regular triangle). Some have just one or two.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.13 Lesson — U13_shapes/L1_describe_polygons
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Naming Polygons by Sides
learn.content: Polygon names tell you the number of sides: TRIANGLE = 3 sides ('tri' = 3). QUADRILATERAL = 4 sides ('quad' = 4). PENTAGON = 5 sides ('penta' = 5). HEXAGON = 6 sides ('hex' = 6). OCTAGON = 8 sides ('oct' = 8). Tip: an octopus has 8 arms — octagon has 8 sides.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.13 Lesson — U13_shapes/L1_describe_polygons
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Polygon or Not? — The Two Tests
learn.content: To check if a figure is a polygon, ask TWO questions: (1) Is it CLOSED? If there is any gap or opening, it is NOT a polygon. (2) Are ALL sides STRAIGHT? If even ONE side is curved, it is NOT a polygon. A circle fails test 2 (curved). An open V-shape fails test 1 (not closed).

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.13 Lesson — U13_shapes/L1_describe_polygons
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Lesson Summary
learn.content: Today you learned: (1) A polygon is a CLOSED figure with all STRAIGHT sides. (2) SIDES are the straight segments. (3) VERTICES are the corners where sides meet. (4) ANGLES are formed at each vertex. (5) For any polygon: sides = vertices = angles. (6) A RIGHT ANGLE is a square corner. (7) Names: 3=triangle, 4=quadrilateral, 5=pentagon, 6=hexagon, 8=octagon.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.13 Lesson — U13_shapes/L1_describe_polygons
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

try.question: Which describes a figure that IS a polygon?
choices: A) A closed figure with one curved side and three straight sides
B) A closed figure with five straight sides
C) An open figure with four straight sides
solution_steps: Step 1: Test 1 — Is it closed? Yes.
Step 2: Test 2 — Are all sides straight? Yes (5 straight sides).
Step 3: Both tests pass, so it IS a polygon.

A.note: One curved side fails test 2 — not a polygon

C.note: Open figure fails test 1 — not a polygon

D.note: A circle is curved — not a polygon

feedback.correct: Yes! Closed AND all straight = polygon. 5 straight sides means it is a pentagon.
feedback.incorrect: A polygon needs to be CLOSED with ALL STRAIGHT sides. Only choice B passes both tests.

verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015
verification.original_ref: Polygon = closed + all straight. B is correct.

try.feedback_correct: Yes! Closed AND all straight = polygon. 5 straight sides means it is a pentagon.

try.question: Which polygon has 4 right angles?
solution_steps: Step 1: A right angle is a square corner.
Step 2: A square has 4 corners, all of them right angles.
Step 3: Triangles, pentagons, and regular hexagons do not have 4 right angles.

A.note: Triangles only have 3 angles, and most have no right angles

C.note: A regular pentagon has no right angles

D.note: A regular hexagon has no right angles

feedback.correct: Right! A square has 4 right angles — all four corners are square corners.
feedback.incorrect: A square has 4 right angles — every corner is a square corner.

practice_r3.question: Lily says, 'My polygon has more sides than a pentagon but fewer sides than an octagon.' Which polygon could Lily have drawn? (Choose any one that fits.)
solution_steps: Step 1: Pentagon has 5 sides; octagon has 8 sides.
Step 2: Lily's polygon has more than 5 and fewer than 8 — so 6 or 7.
Step 3: Among the choices, only hexagon (6 sides) fits.

feedback.correct: Right! Hexagon has 6 sides, which is between 5 and 8.
feedback.incorrect: Lily's polygon has between 5 and 8 sides. Only the hexagon (6 sides) fits.

practice_r3.question: A polygon has the same number of sides as a hexagon plus a triangle. How many sides does it have?
solution_steps: Step 1: Hexagon = 6 sides.
Step 2: Triangle = 3 sides.
Step 3: 6 + 3 = 9 sides.

feedback.correct: Right! 6 + 3 = 9 sides.
feedback.incorrect: Hexagon (6) + triangle (3) = 9 sides.

practice_r3.question: A polygon has 4 sides AND 4 right angles. Which of these CANNOT describe it?
choices: C) A trapezoid with no right angles
D) Any quadrilateral with 4 square corners
solution_steps: Step 1: The polygon must have 4 sides AND 4 right angles.
Step 2: A square fits — 4 sides, 4 right angles.
Step 3: A rectangle fits — 4 sides, 4 right angles.
Step 4: A trapezoid with NO right angles cannot fit (has 0 right angles, not 4).

A.note: A square does fit — 4 sides and 4 right angles

B.note: A rectangle fits — 4 sides and 4 right angles

D.note: Any 4-sided shape with 4 square corners fits the description

feedback.correct: Right! A trapezoid with no right angles has 0 right angles, not 4 — so it cannot match.
feedback.incorrect: The polygon must have 4 right angles. A trapezoid with no right angles fails that requirement.

practice_r3.three_read_prompt: Read 1: What two rules must the polygon follow? Read 2: Which choices follow both rules? Read 3: Which choice fails one rule?

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.13 Lesson — U13_shapes/L1_describe_polygons
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015
verification.original_ref: Trapezoid with 0 right angles fails 'must have 4 right angles'.

hints: Check each choice against both rules: 4 sides AND 4 right angles.
practice_r3.feedback_correct: Right! A trapezoid with no right angles has 0 right angles, not 4 — so it cannot match.

practice_r3.question: Two polygons together have 11 sides. One is a quadrilateral. How many sides does the OTHER polygon have?
solution_steps: Step 1: Quadrilateral = 4 sides.
Step 2: Total = 11 sides. Other polygon's sides = 11 - 4 = 7.
Step 3: A 7-sided polygon is a heptagon.

11.note: Don't include the quadrilateral's sides — subtract them

4.note: That's the quadrilateral, not the other polygon

feedback.correct: Right! 11 − 4 = 7 sides.
feedback.incorrect: Quadrilateral has 4 sides. 11 − 4 = 7. The other polygon has 7 sides.

practice_r3.three_read_prompt: Read 1: What is the total? Read 2: How many sides does a quadrilateral have? Read 3: What operation finds the other polygon's sides?

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.13 Lesson — U13_shapes/L1_describe_polygons
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

hints: Subtract the quadrilateral's 4 sides from the total of 11.

practice_r3.question: Ben says, 'I drew a polygon with 3 sides and 4 vertices.' Could Ben be correct?
choices: A) Yes — sides and vertices can differ
B) Yes — only if the figure is curved
C) No — sides and vertices must be equal in any polygon
D) No — polygons must have at least 4 sides
solution_steps: Step 1: In every polygon, sides = vertices.
Step 2: Ben said 3 sides and 4 vertices — that is impossible.
Step 3: A polygon with 3 sides has exactly 3 vertices.

A.note: Sides and vertices are always equal in a polygon

B.note: Polygons cannot be curved at all

feedback.correct: Right! Sides always equal vertices. 3 sides means 3 vertices, not 4.
feedback.incorrect: Sides and vertices are ALWAYS equal in a polygon. Ben must be mistaken.

practice_r3.three_read_prompt: Read 1: What did Ben say? Read 2: What is the rule for sides and vertices? Read 3: Does Ben's claim match the rule?

hints: What is the rule connecting sides and vertices in any polygon?
practice_r3.feedback_correct: Right! Sides always equal vertices. 3 sides means 3 vertices, not 4.
